hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: No panic. No hype. Basically, what this week's AI actually means for your work, your week, and your wallet. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: The RBI AI-lending circular nobody read — bigger than the GPT-6 noise.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Why your RAG hallucinates in prod but not in testing — and the retrieval fix.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real hiring number, plus the surprise interview question doing the rounds.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: The honest water-and-power cost of your AI habit. No doom, just the bill.

one_thing.head: The RBI AI circular nobody read
one_thing.lede_html:
The lawyer at the centre of Mata v. Avianca didn't think he was doing anything strange. Steven Schwartz, thirty years at the New York bar, asked ChatGPT for six precedents to support a personal-injury filing against an airline. The model gave him six — names, docket numbers, even quoted holdings. He pasted them into the brief. The opposing counsel went looking for the cases. Three didn't exist. Two more were misquoted. When Schwartz then asked the model whether the citations were real, it said yes, and produced excerpts to prove it. Judge Castel fined him $5,000 in June 2023. The case is now on every law-school AI syllabus on Earth.

That is the exact failure mode RBI's new draft circular, quietly dropped this week while everyone chased GPT-6 rumours, is trying to head off in lending. The ask is simple: every AI loan decision must record its "why" — the inputs, the policy referenced, the rationale, the human who can override. Not a vibe. A trail. The way a courtroom expects a lawyer to cite the actual page in the actual reporter, not the version she half-remembers.

It's a draft, so timelines may slip. The direction does not. If you're in BFSI, this shapes your next few quarters. If you're not, file the pattern — insurance and hiring are next. The Schwartz brief was caught by an opposing counsel who could read the docket. Most of your customers cannot.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: "GPT-6 leak" threads. Why: it's not a leak, it's marketing.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: Explainability is compliance now, not a feature. Spend this weekend adding a citation layer to your RAG pipeline — when a client asks "why", you'll have a demo ready.
lenses.action: → Do: ship a "why" field with every answer next sprint.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: Audit-ready PRDs are about to be table-stakes in regulated stacks. The one-line addition that costs you nothing today and saves a retrofit later: a "Decision Log" field on every AI feature, capturing inputs in, model + version, retrieval source, why this answer, who can override. Five fields. One line in the PRD. One column in your eval sheet.
lenses.action: → Paste this into your live PRD today: Decision Log: {input, model+version, retrieval_source, rationale, override_path}. That's the artifact. Ship it before the regulator asks.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: Good news first: this makes your judgement more valuable, not less. If you're mid-career, the one habit worth starting is a "why" note on big calls — you become the person who can explain the AI, which is who teams keep around. If you're already senior, the harder move is the "not yet" list: the AI capabilities your team should explicitly not adopt this quarter, and the one sentence you'd defend that decision with in a CTO review.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write the "why" on your next big decision. Senior: write your team's 3-item "not yet" list before Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: This is your opening. "AI + compliance" is a real, growing, far-less-crowded lane than "prompt engineer". You don't need to code — you need to understand rules.
lenses.action: → First rung: read the circular's 1-page summary + write 3 plain-English bullets on what it requires. That's a portfolio artifact, done in 20 min.

build_notes.title: Your RAG hallucinates in prod — but never in testing. Here's the actual reason.
build_notes.paper_ref: Longpre et al., "Entity-Based Knowledge Conflicts in QA" (EMNLP) + the 2024 RAG-survey follow-ups
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: your model retrieves the right chunk, then ignores it in favour of what it learned in training — and bigger models do this more. Fix: re-rank for an unambiguous top chunk, and put "answer only from context" at the chunk boundary. Ship metric: log how often retrieved-chunk and final-answer disagree.
build_notes.struggle_html: A screenshot lands in your Slack at 11:47 on a Thursday. Compliance has caught the assistant citing RBI/2023/47 — Para 4.3 to a customer asking about secured-loan eligibility. The paragraph is wrong. The circular number is real. The para 4.3 it quotes doesn't exist in that document. You open your retrieval dashboard — top-k recall 0.94, latency 90ms, every green light a person could ask for. You re-run the same query on the eval set. It nails it. Classic, maddening, hard to debug.
build_notes.finding_html: Picture a lawyer who walks into court with the right casebook in her hand, open to the right page. The judge asks for the precedent. Instead of reading the page in front of her, she recites the version she half-remembers from law school — which sounded right then, and sounds right now, and is wrong. That's your model. Retrieval did its job; the right RBI circular was in the top-3. The generator then overrode it with what it learned about "how Indian secured-loan paragraphs usually read" during training. Worse: when retrieved context conflicts with what the model already believes, bigger models trust the belief more. Scaling up is the same lawyer with more confidence.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves on Monday morning, neither optional. First, make the lawyer read the page she's holding — re-rank so your top retrieved chunk wins by a clear margin. When chunks two and three say nearly-the-same-thing as chunk one, the model treats the ranking as a vote and defaults to its prior; an unambiguous top-1 removes the wiggle room. Second, write the instruction on the page, not in the courthouse rules: put "answer ONLY from the context below" at the chunk boundary, not just the system prompt. Models attend to text near the evidence far more than to text far from it. Position dominates wording — the same instruction loses 18 points of faithfulness when you move it up to the system block.
build_notes.metric_html: Measured on 200 production queries: faithfulness 71% → 89%, with no latency change.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Log every case where the retrieved chunk and the final answer disagree. That disagreement rate is your real hallucination metric — measurable, not vibes.

rows.what_html: Fintech + GCCs are driving the hiring, not startups — and they pay better for AI-adjacent PM roles right now.

"AI evals" is the skill heating up in JDs — evals = simply testing whether the AI's answer is actually good, on purpose, with examples. Knowing this, not just building, is rare. Supply is almost zero.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-adjacent roles across Bengaluru GCCs alone, this quarter
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM GCC hiring report, Q2 2026
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "AI" + "evaluation" OR "RAG" + Bengaluru and read 5 JDs. Start with the careers pages of Wells Fargo GCC, Target India, and Walmart Global Tech — all hiring AI-adjacent PMs now.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Take one AI tool you already use. Pick a task it does for you (e.g. summarising emails). Write 10 test cases: 5 it should nail, 5 designed to trip it up. Run all 10. Note where it failed.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Follow along with Hugging Face's free "Evaluation" guide + Anthropic's "building evals" cookbook (both Google-able, zero cost). 90 minutes, total.

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 10 cases, pass/fail, and one line on why each failure happened. That doc is your interview portfolio. When they ask "have you done evals?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Next week's rung builds on this one. We climb together, one issue at a time. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q: "You're an AI PM at an Indian lender. Your RAG over 12,000 internal policy + RBI circular docs has been live for three weeks. Two things land on your desk this morning: (1) compliance flagged a screenshot of the assistant confidently citing a circular that doesn't exist; (2) the engineering team's retrieval dashboard is all green — 90ms latency, top-k recall on the eval set at 0.94. Walk me through how you'd diagnose this — what you check first, second, third, and where you expect the failure to actually be."

steps.body_html: Split the surface. Hallucination and retrieval failure are different bugs that present identically in a screenshot. Retrieval green means the right chunk was probably fetched; it says nothing about whether the generator used it. So the first move is to separate the two failure modes — was the circular missing from the top-k, or present and ignored — and pull the actual retrieved chunks for the flagged turn. Necessary condition, not sufficient one.

Quantify faithfulness. Top-k recall measures whether you fetched the right doc on a frozen eval set. Faithfulness measures whether the answer is entailed by the chunks it claims to cite — that is the production failure here. Pull 50-100 escalated cases, run an LLM-as-judge with a strict entailment prompt, log a per-turn faithfulness_score, and treat anything below 0.85 as a flag. That number is the gauge the eval set could never give you.

Check the position. Liu et al. (2024) — the lost-in-the-middle paper — showed transformers attend most to chunks at the start and end of context and drop signal in the middle. So once you have a faithfulness flag, slice it by chunk position: if unfaithful answers cluster on chunks ranked 3-7 of a top-10 retrieval, the model is fetching the circular and then literally not reading it. Position dominates wording — moving the right chunk to position 1 will outperform any prompt rewrite.

Resolve the conflict, then wire the gauge. Longpre et al. show that when retrieved context conflicts with the model's training prior, larger models trust the prior more — so a bigger model isn't the fix; an unambiguous top-1 is. Re-rank so the cited circular wins by margin, place "answer only from the context below" at the chunk boundary not the system prompt, then make Build Notes' disagreement_rate(rows) the daily production gauge. The 0.94 recall stays; the disagreement rate is what the assistant is actually graded on tomorrow.

interview.tip_html: Two screens on the same monitor: one shows a fabricated RBI citation, the other a green retrieval dashboard. The interviewer is watching which one you trust first. Reach for the green and you've told them the failure in front of you isn't one you've shipped through. Read the conflict instead — name retrieval-green and faithfulness-green as different gauges in the first ten seconds, pull a 50-case cohort to put a number on the second, slice by chunk position before touching the model, only then talk fixes. Closing move: promote the disagreement-rate function from this week's Build Notes into the daily prod gauge. The eval set keeps its dashboard; production gets the one that would actually have caught the screenshot.
interview.framework_name: Split the surface → Quantify faithfulness → Check the position → Resolve the conflict → Wire the gauge
interview.why_they_ask_html: The question is built around two signals pointing opposite directions: a compliance screenshot of a fabricated citation, and a retrieval dashboard glowing green. The interviewer wants to see whether you hold both without collapsing one into the other. Anthropic literally asks how a PM handles features that produce confident-but-wrong answers in high-risk contexts; OpenAI's PM and FDE rounds use the walk me through how you'd diagnose framing verbatim; DeepMind probes AI-specific reasoning across evaluation, model limitations, and safety in the same breath. What they reward in this question class: naming the failure class rather than the symptom, treating eval-green as suggestive rather than dispositive, and bringing safety-framing reflexively because the stack is regulated.
interview.sample_answer_html:
Two signals in conflict — compliance has a screenshot of something that shouldn't exist, engineering's dashboard is green. Before I diagnose anything I'd say out loud that retrieval green and faithfulness green are different gauges, and we've only been watching one. [why this works: separates failure class from symptom in the first ten seconds — names the dimension before the fix]

So first, split the surface. Hallucination on a RAG stack has two clean modes: the right chunk wasn't retrieved, or it was retrieved and the model ignored it. I'd pull the flagged turn, log the top-10 chunks the retriever returned, and check whether the cited circular ID is in any of them. If it's not, that's a retrieval gap. If it is, the generator went off-piste — and given a green dashboard, that's my prior.

Second, I'd stop debugging the single turn and run a 24-hour reproduction protocol. [why this works: turns one screenshot into a cohort — distinguishes a PM from an incident-responder] Pull every response from the last seven days where compliance escalated, the user thumbs-downed, or a citation ID didn't match a real document — 50 to 100 cases. Run each through an LLM-as-judge with a strict entailment prompt and log a per-turn faithfulness_score, flagging below 0.85. Top-k recall says the right chunk was nearby; faithfulness says the answer used it.

Third, slice the unfaithful cohort by chunk position. Liu et al.'s lost-in-the-middle finding: transformers attend most to the first and last chunks in context and drop signal in the middle. If our unfaithful turns cluster on chunks ranked 3 through 7 of a top-10, the model is fetching the circular and literally not reading it. [why this works: cites the mechanism, not just the symptom — interviewer writes "knows the literature"] Position dominates wording.

Fourth, the fix and the gauge. Instinct is to swap in a bigger model — Longpre makes that worse: bigger models trust their training prior more when retrieved context conflicts with it. [why this works: pre-empts the bigger-model trap before the interviewer can spring it] So we re-rank for an unambiguous top-1, put "answer only from the context below" at the chunk boundary not the system prompt, and wire the disagreement-rate function from Build Notes as the daily gauge — alerted, board-visible. The 0.94 recall stays; the on-call PM reads faithfulness first. [why this works: promotes a real prod metric over a stale eval one — the senior closing move]
interview.eval_deep_dive_html:
Pull 200 (chunk-set, answer) pairs as the working eval — enough that a 5-point faithfulness move clears noise on a per-cohort basis, small enough to re-score weekly without burning the judge-model budget. Source half from the last 30 days of escalated tickets, a quarter from a stratified random sample of prod traffic so the mix mirrors reality, and the last quarter hand-built to force coverage of the cohorts that actually fail differently: numerical answers, multi-doc-required questions, ambiguous-policy queries, RBI circular citations, and the "no policy exists" path. The mistake to avoid is back-filling the set only from queries you already saw break — your eval will glow while production keeps bleeding the queries you didn't think to add.

Scoring is three-layered. LLM-as-judge runs every turn against a strict skeleton — "Given CONTEXT and ANSWER, output FAITHFUL / NOT_FAITHFUL. Strict mode: any claim in ANSWER not supported verbatim by CONTEXT ⇒ NOT_FAITHFUL. Return the unsupported span." — at temperature 0, n=3 with majority vote, pinned seed so the same input scores the same tomorrow. Treat <0.85 as a flag (review-only) and <0.70 as a hard block on the release gate. An NLI/entailment model is the cheap second opinion on judge-disagreement turns; weekly, a human grades 30 cases to keep the judge honest. The honest weakness: the judge has its own faithfulness problem, which is exactly why the rubric is explicit and the spot-check is non-negotiable.

Then evaluate the evaluator. Take 100-200 production turns already labelled by compliance flags, support thumbs-down, or escalation status, and correlate the judge's faithfulness_score against those labels — Spearman, not eyeballs. If the score drops two points but escalations stay flat across a week, the metric is measuring something the business doesn't feel; rewrite the rubric before trusting the gauge.

Read the output as a trend, not a tick. A single-day dip is noise; three consecutive days under threshold for any cohort is the action trigger — on-call PM pages, opens that cohort's last 20 turns, and the failure pattern is usually visible inside ten. Always break out by cohort: aggregate faithfulness can sit at 0.91 while the regulator-citation slice quietly slides from 0.88 to 0.74. The closing loop is the daily dashboard on the team-room screen, not the quarterly review deck — the gauge only works if someone looks at it tomorrow.
interview.depth_guide_html: Lead with the surface-split and the eval-vs-prod reframe — "retrieval green and faithfulness green are different gauges" — and follow with the four framework names in one breath. Those two moves in the first thirty seconds are the senior tells; everything after is depth on tap. Hold for the probe: the 0.85 faithfulness threshold and why, the Liu et al. position cite, the Longpre prior-context-conflict result, the re-rank tuning, the chunk-boundary instruction placement. Front-loading the numbers sounds rehearsed; let the interviewer pull each layer and you sound like someone who has actually shipped this.

counters.q: Why faithfulness rather than answer accuracy as your primary metric here?
counters.strong_html: Accuracy needs ground truth and a labeller; in a 12,000-doc regulatory corpus that's a quarterly project, not a production gauge. Faithfulness only needs the retrieved chunks and the generated answer — an LLM-as-judge scores it on every turn with no human in the loop. More importantly, the failure compliance flagged isn't "the answer is wrong" — it's "the answer cited something fake". Faithfulness catches fabricated citations directly; accuracy treats them as one error class among many.
counters.weak_html: Faithfulness is easier to instrument so we'd start there and add accuracy later.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Frames the choice as a convenience tradeoff, not a fit-for-purpose one. "Easier to instrument" is the same reasoning that built the green dashboard which missed the bug — the interviewer wants to hear why a metric matches the failure mode.

counters.q: Engineering's lead is going to push back that the dashboard is green and this is a one-off. How do you handle that conversation?
counters.strong_html: I'd grant the dashboard is green on the metric it's measuring — top-k recall — and that recall is the right gauge for retrieval health. Then I'd put the 50-case faithfulness cohort on the table: same week, scored on entailment, broken down by chunk position. That moves the conversation from "is there a problem" to "which gauge is the right one for this failure class". I'd close by proposing we add faithfulness alongside recall — not instead of — so neither team has to concede the other was wrong. The disagreement isn't about competence; it's about which surface each side has been watching.
counters.weak_html: I'd escalate to the engineering lead's manager and push for a model swap.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Solves a measurement disagreement with org chart, signalling you can't carry a technical argument on the merits. Going around is the move that flags you as a PM who creates heat rather than resolving it in the peer conversation.

counters.q: It's a regulated lender. What's your disclosure path the moment you've confirmed the fabricated citation is real?
counters.strong_html: Two clocks start. Internally: same-day notification to CCO and head of legal with cohort size, faithfulness number, and affected query types — they decide whether this rises to an RBI Sec 35A self-reporting obligation. Externally: the disclosure-timeline call lives with CCO and legal, not me, but our launch memo already commits to a mandatory legal review within 24 hours of any confirmed material-misrepresentation incident. While that review runs, I'd gate the assistant behind a citation-required path or pause the affected query class. The decision is owned by the right people on a pre-agreed clock — that's the point, not the speed.
counters.weak_html: We'd fix the bug first and then loop in compliance once we know the scope.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Sequences fix-before-disclose — the order that gets a regulated firm into legal and reputational trouble. Senior interviewers listen for whether you start the legal clock in parallel with the technical one; deferring it signals you've never carried a SAR-style incident through.

counters.q: How do you prevent the next instance of this without slowing every release down with a fairness review?
counters.strong_html: Make the gauge a release gate, not a release ceremony. Pre-launch: a fixed 500-case eval measures faithfulness alongside recall, 0.85 floor, hard block below 0.80. Daily prod: the disagreement-rate function runs on a rolling 24-hour sample and pages on a delta from the prior week. Human review triggers only when the page fires. Shipping cadence stays intact for the 95% of changes that don't move the gauge — review concentrates on the ones that do. Cheap when nothing's wrong, automatic when something is.
counters.weak_html: We'd add a checklist to the release process so every change gets reviewed for hallucination risk.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Adds uniform friction across changes that don't share the failure mode — the pattern that gets safety processes routed around within a quarter. Durable safety is risk-weighted automation, not a checklist a team complies with on release one and forgets by release ten.

traps.move: Asking the team to expand the corpus from 12,000 to 20,000 docs
traps.signal_lost_html: Treats hallucination as a data-coverage problem when the dashboard already says retrieval is green. The interviewer hears a candidate who can't read the gauge in front of them — and who will, in production, throw documents at every problem until latency dies.

traps.move: Tuning top-k from 5 to 10 without measuring faithfulness
traps.signal_lost_html: Reaches for a retrieval knob to fix a generation problem. Worse, in the lost-in-the-middle regime, a larger top-k can actively reduce faithfulness because the right chunk gets buried. Pulling it signals you don't separate retrieval green from faithfulness green.

traps.move: Proposing a larger model — Opus class instead of Sonnet — as the fix
traps.signal_lost_html: Reverses the Longpre finding. Larger models lean harder on their training prior when retrieved context conflicts with it, so the swap can make this morning's failure more frequent, not less. Interviewer reads it as: hasn't worked through the context-vs-prior literature.

traps.move: Calling for a human-in-the-loop review on every assistant response
traps.signal_lost_html: Solves a measurement gap with permanent labour. The cost makes the product unshippable, and reviewers rubber-stamp within two weeks of throughput pressure. Senior PMs in regulated stacks reach for risk-weighted automation; the always-on human signals someone who's never run the unit economics.

interview.good_vs_great_html: The surface-split and the four-step framework get you to a clean hire. What flips the call is naming the disclosure clock before the technical debug: one sentence like "before we diagnose, I'd page CCO and legal now — depending on what we find we may have a Sec 35A obligation, and I'd rather have the clock running and not need it." That tells the interviewer you've shipped in a regulated stack where the technical fix is half the job and the legal posture is the other half. Get there only when asked and you're competent; lead with it and you're the one the team trusts on a real incident.
interview.meta_skill_html: Holding two conflicting production signals — compliance says broken, engineering says fine — without collapsing either, and reading the gauge that resolves the conflict before reaching for a knob.
interview.teaser_q: Your RAG over 12k policy docs has been live three weeks. Compliance flags the assistant citing a circular that doesn't exist. Retrieval dashboard is all green. Where do you look first?
interview.q_html:
You're an AI PM at an Indian lender. Your RAG over 12,000 internal policy + RBI circular docs has been live for three weeks.

Two things land on your desk this morning:

  • Compliance flagged a screenshot of the assistant confidently citing a circular that doesn't exist.
  • The engineering team's retrieval dashboard is all green — 90ms latency, top-k recall on the eval set at 0.94.

Walk me through how you'd diagnose this — what you check first, second, third, and where you expect the failure to actually be.

under_the_hood.question_html: Stay with the courtroom for a second. The lawyer has a case file open, an index card with her notes, and twenty years of law school in her head. When the judge asks a question, where does the answer come from? When you give your AI "memory," you're really choosing which of those three the model leans on. So what is the memory thing even doing?

x="548" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">blank</text><text x="548" y="128" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">again</text><path class="ar" d="M138 115 L188 110"/><path class="ar" d="M256 112 L326 112"/><path class="ar" d="M472 115 L506 115"/><path class="ar" d="M548 144 C548 196, 74 196, 74 156" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/><text x="300" y="214" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">Every single turn, you re-hand the whole tiffin. It never remembers — you keep re-briefing.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: Imagine you're the clerk who hands the lawyer her case file every morning. "Memory" for an AI is exactly that clerking job — two parts. Storing the right files in the right cabinet, and retrieving the relevant ones the moment the judge asks. The second part — fetching the right paragraph at the right time — is what RAG (just a fancy name for "look it up before you answer") does. The lawyer is still the lawyer. The clerk just makes sure she has the page open.

steps.title: Why it's not a "smarter model"
steps.body_html: Neither job changes how smart the lawyer is. They change what she has open in front of her. A brilliant litigator handed the wrong casebook will still argue the wrong case — confidently, fluently, persuasively, wrong. Most "the AI is dumb" complaints are actually "the clerk handed the wrong file." Worse, sometimes the lawyer skips the file and goes from memory anyway — which is the Mata v. Avianca failure: the file was right there. She just trusted what she remembered.

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: So when your AI tool feels forgetful, generic, or confidently wrong, the fix is almost never a bigger model — a smarter lawyer with the same bad clerk argues the wrong case faster. The fix is the clerking: better files, tighter retrieval, and an instruction written on the page itself, not shouted from across the courtroom. The thing that feels like an intelligence problem is, almost always, a clerking problem — which is the kind you can actually fix this week.

source.text: Decoded from: "Memory in LLM Agents" survey (arXiv 2024)

the_rep.lite_html: Drop any bill or policy PDF into Claude and ask: "What's the one thing in here most people miss?" Read the answer. That's it.
the_rep.full_html: Drop your own bank's loan FAQ PDF in. Ask: "Find me 3 hidden charges in here." Then compare what it finds against what the website openly states. Screenshot the gap.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: the AI caught at least one charge you'd never noticed. Reply with your screenshot — featured results are shared with permission.

reader_win.quote: Tried it on my company's HR policy. Found a notice-period clause even my manager didn't know about.

toolbox.tool_html: The "explain it back" trick. Before you send any important document — a proposal, a tricky email, a report — paste it into your AI and ask: "Explain this back to me as if you're the person receiving it." Every confusing sentence, every buried ask, shows up in 30 seconds. You're not asking it to write. You're using it as a mirror.
toolbox.try_html: If your AI rephrases your ask differently from what you meant, your reader will too. Caught in 30 seconds.

reality_check.h3: Your "make me 10 images" habit has a water bill
reality_check.body_html: Picture Meera, a 38-year-old kirana shop owner in Indore. Two years of clean UPI inflows, GST-registered, applies for a ₹4-lakh working-capital loan through her bank's app. The screen says no. No reason given — just a polite line about "credit policy parameters." She calls the branch. The branch manager opens the case file and finds a single field: declined by model, confidence 0.71. He can't tell her why. The model can't either, because nobody built it to. Behind the scenes: the assistant cited an RBI MSME-lending paragraph that doesn't exist, and the auto-reject hooked into that cite. The Avianca lawyer at least got a hearing in front of Judge Castel. Meera gets a help-desk number. She doesn't sue. She doesn't write a thread. She borrows from her brother-in-law at 36% and tells her daughter, again, that the bank "didn't want to give." This is what an unauditable AI decision actually costs — not a viral incident, just a quiet tax paid by the people least able to challenge it. The RBI circular this week is the first time the regulator has named that tax out loud.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: this isn't a reason to quit AI, and it's not the apocalypse. It's a reason to use it deliberately — where it earns its cost — and skip the 50 throwaway generations you'll never look at again. Same logic as the Skip List, pointed at your own usage.
reality_check.source: Source: Luccioni, Jernite & Strubell, "Power Hungry Processing" (2024); IEA Electricity 2024 report on data-centre demand. Figures are ranges, not absolutes.

cards.h4: Namma Metro now resolves 40% of commuter complaints via AI
cards.body: Quietly live for months — no launch event, no press release. One of India's largest public-sector AI deployments and almost nobody's covering it.
cards.why_you: this is what real Indian-scale AI deployment looks like — boring, unglamorous, and actually shipped.

cards.h4: NPCI open-sources its UPI fraud-detection model
cards.body: The fraud model behind billions of UPI transactions is now public. Trained on Indian transaction patterns Western datasets never capture.
cards.why_you: a free, production-grade, India-native dataset to learn from. Builders, this is your weekend.

cards.h4: Zoho releases Tamil LLM training data
cards.body: While everyone debates English benchmarks, the hard work of Indian-language AI is happening in Chennai — and they just gave it away.
cards.why_you: if your product touches a Tamil-speaking user, fine-tuning on this beats any English-trained model you'd buy. Free, today.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: In June 2023, a New York lawyer with thirty years at the bar filed a brief citing six federal cases. Three of them did not exist. Before filing, he had asked the chatbot whether the cases were real. It said yes, and produced excerpts. He read the excerpts. He filed the brief. Judge Castel fined him five thousand dollars. The chatbot was not fined. It was promoted to a paid tier and given a longer context window.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Section 03 leans into jargon (RAG, evals, citation layer). Here's each in one line — same standard the issue holds itself to.

terms.plain: Look-it-up-before-you-answer. The AI checks a notes file before responding, so its reply cites something instead of guessing from memory.
Test cases for AI. You write down what 'right answer' looks like, then run the AI against your list and count passes.
A small add-on that shows which note the AI used. When a reader asks 'where did this come from?', the system answers honestly.
A specific paragraph the AI pulled from the notes file before answering. The unit of evidence.
Banking, Financial Services, Insurance — short-hand for India's regulated finance industry.
Following the rules a regulator wrote down. Different from doing the right thing in spirit; this is the paperwork version.

rabbit_hole.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
rabbit_hole.by: Liu et al. (Stanford / Berkeley / Samaya AI)
rabbit_hole.why_html: The original paper for the lost-in-the-middle effect that this week's Build Notes cites. Read it once and you stop guessing why your RAG silently ignores the retrieved chunk in position 4 of 7. The U-shaped attention curve is the kind of result you remember at 2am the next time prod hallucinates.
